concepts_addressed: Market Selection Criteria: Growth + Pain + Targeting

frameworks.name: Three Macro Opportunity Waves
frameworks.definition: A sequence of three platform-level macro opportunities — Internet, Web 2.0 (social), and AI — each spawning sub-layer micro opportunities (e.g., Google Search, Facebook Ads, TikTok Shop) with outsized early-mover returns before market stabilization.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that there have been three macro-level platform opportunities in his lifetime: the Internet, Web 2.0 (social media), and AI — each representing a wave where early participants captured disproportionate returns before the market stabilized.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that TikTok Shop (as of 2025-2026) is in a land-grab phase analogous to Amazon in 2013-14, where a supply-demand imbalance — more influencers wanting to earn money than products available to sell — allows new participants with no prior track record to scale to $1M/month within 60-90 days.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Participant enters during the current supply-demand imbalance window before the market stabilizes
claims.mechanism: Supply-demand discrepancy: more influencers wanting revenue than products available to sell creates temporarily outsized returns for anyone offering products

numbers.axis: time to reach $1M/month revenue for new entrants

bounded_by: Returns compress once supply of products catches up with influencer demand and the market stabilizes

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that land-grab market windows — periods of acute supply-demand imbalance in a new platform or channel — produce outsized returns that are unavailable once the market stabilizes; therefore, capturing them requires both recognizing the window and entering it early.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Participant enters before market equilibrium is reached
claims.mechanism: When supply (products, sellers, operators) cannot yet meet demand (platform audience, buyers, traffic), early entrants earn abnormal margins until equilibrium restores competitive pricing
bounded_by: Once the market stabilizes, returns regress to normal competitive levels

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that possessing skills is the non-negotiable prerequisite for capturing any macro or micro opportunity; without skills, access to a large platform or audience does not translate into outsized income.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: Skills determine whether a person can convert an opportunity window into revenue; without execution capability, the window passes unused

claims.text: Alex Hormozi observed that social media brand-building was a more powerful opportunity when platforms like Instagram launched (circa 2010-2012), when simply posting lifestyle content could attract millions of followers and monetize them; as of his statement he judges that opportunity as still real but less pronounced than at launch.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Creator quality matters more in the mature social media era than at platform launch
bounded_by: In early Instagram era, minimal skill (sunset photos) sufficed for massive reach; in the mature era, being good is required

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that being able to build a brand and get paid to do it via social media remains an extraordinary opportunity that he finds mind-boggling still exists, even in the mature era of social media.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Creator must be skilled enough to build audience in competitive social media environment

claims.text: Alex Hormozi states that Acquisition.com has developed a 10-stage roadmap from zero to $100M+ revenue, broken down across eight business functions, and that fewer than 1% of companies complete all stages; he claims to have done it multiple times across software, physical products, service businesses, and brick-and-mortar.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi

numbers.axis: completion rate of the 10-stage scaling roadmap

examples.text: Alex Hormozi recounts that he knows multiple people who had never achieved significant business results before, and then within 60-90 days of entering TikTok Shop were generating $1M/month in revenue. He attributes this not to their skill or prior track record but entirely to the supply-demand imbalance on the platform at that moment — more influencers wanting to monetize than products available to sell through them.

In a conversation with the interviewer, Alex Hormozi recalled telling him early on: if he had the interviewer's following, he estimated he would have become a billionaire — framing the interviewer's audience as a supply-demand event (large audience supply, limited skilled operators to monetize it) that Hormozi could have exploited with his skills. This illustrates his thesis that skills are the multiplier that converts an audience or platform opportunity into revenue.

Alex Hormozi references Amazon circa 2013-14 as an historical analogue to the current TikTok Shop window — a period when entering the platform was easy, competition was thin, and simply participating produced outsized returns that were unavailable to those who entered after the market matured.

practices.text: Look for micro opportunities (e.g., TikTok Shop, Google Search at launch, Facebook Ads early) nested within macro waves (Internet, Web 2.0, AI) and enter them before supply catches up with demand.
Acquire skills before or during the land-grab window so you are positioned to execute when the opportunity presents itself.
